resume:
CLINICAL RESEARCH & QUALITY-IMPROVEMENT INTERN CANDIDATE
Third-year Bachelor of Science in Nursing (BSN) student | Pre-licensure | Anticipated graduation next spring

OBJECTIVE
A highly motivated pre-licensure nursing student seeking a summer clinical quality-improvement internship in an acute-care setting. I am drawn to the intersection of bedside care, patient safety, and data, and I want to spend the summer learning how a hospital actually measures and improves outcomes rather than just reading about it in a textbook. Over the past two years I have deliberately stacked my clinical rotations, a faculty research assistantship, and a student-led quality project so that I would arrive with more than classroom theory, and I am genuinely comfortable being the most junior person in the room and asking a lot of questions until I understand the why behind a process, not just the steps.

CLINICAL ROTATIONS (in chronological order)
Riverside Regional Medical Center — Medical-Surgical Unit (fall, sophomore year, 120 hours)
My first hospital rotation. I shadowed and then assisted RNs with vitals, ambulation, intake and output charting, and basic wound care under direct supervision. This is where I learned to give a structured handoff using SBAR, and where I first watched a charge nurse triage competing demands across a busy 28-bed unit and realized how much invisible prioritization happens every shift. I kept a small notebook of every workflow that seemed inefficient, which is probably where my interest in quality improvement first took root.
Riverside Regional Medical Center — Pediatrics (spring, sophomore year, 90 hours)
Worked alongside the pediatric nursing staff, focused on weight-based medication dose checking and family-centered communication. I participated in two rapid-response drills and saw how a calm, well-rehearsed team protocol changes the outcome of a deteriorating patient. Pediatrics also taught me how much careful double-checking matters when the margin for a dosing error is so small.
St. Aldric Community Hospital — Telemetry / Cardiac Step-Down (fall, junior year, 135 hours)
My most recent and most independent rotation to date. I carried a small patient assignment under preceptor supervision, learned to interpret basic rhythm strips, and contributed to discharge teaching for heart-failure patients and their families. This is where my interest in readmission metrics really started, because I kept seeing the same patients come back and wondered how the hospital was tracking and trying to prevent that.
Community Health Outreach — Public Health Clinical (winter intersession, junior year, 60 hours)
Ran blood-pressure screening tables at community events and helped build a low-literacy hypertension education handout that the clinic still hands out. This taught me how differently you have to communicate the same clinical information depending on your audience.

RESEARCH & PROJECT EXPERIENCE
Faculty Research Assistant — College of Nursing (junior year, ongoing, about 8 hours per week)
I assist a faculty principal investigator on a retrospective chart-review study examining fall-risk documentation completeness on two inpatient units. My specific role is extracting de-identified data points into a structured spreadsheet against a detailed codebook, flagging ambiguous or contradictory chart entries for the PI to adjudicate rather than guessing myself, and helping draft the methods section of the eventual manuscript. I have abstracted roughly 200 charts so far and have learned how easy it is to introduce error if you drift from the codebook even slightly. I completed CITI human-subjects research training before I was allowed to touch any patient data.
Student-Led Quality Improvement Project — Hand-Hygiene Compliance (junior year, one semester)
With a team of four nursing students, we designed a small observational audit of hand-hygiene compliance on our clinical unit using a standardized, validated observation tool so our data would be comparable to the unit's own audits. I personally collected about 60 of the roughly 150 total observations and built the run chart that we presented. Compliance in our sample sat around 71 percent, well below the unit's stated 90 percent target, and after walking the unit we proposed a glove-box relocation as a low-cost, low-friction intervention. We presented our findings and the proposal to the unit educator; I honestly do not yet know whether the change was formally adopted, and I would not claim a result I cannot verify.

LEADERSHIP & WORK EXPERIENCE
Certified Nursing Assistant (CNA) — Maplewood Skilled Nursing Facility (two summers and part-time during the school year)
Direct resident care including activities of daily living, vitals, repositioning, and documentation. This is my longest-running real-world care experience and where I genuinely learned the discipline of accurate, timely charting, because in long-term care the chart is often the only continuous memory of a resident.
Peer Tutor — University Anatomy and Physiology Lab
Tutored first- and second-year students and ran weekly review sessions for groups of eight to twelve, which sharpened my ability to explain a complicated process simply.
Treasurer — Student Nurses Association chapter
Managed a small chapter budget and coordinated two fundraisers, keeping the ledger and reconciling it monthly.

SKILLS & CERTIFICATIONS
Basic Life Support (BLS) certified; CNA licensed; CITI human-subjects research certified; comfortable navigating Epic from clinical rotations; intermediate Excel including pivot tables and basic run and control charts; SBAR handoff communication.

A NOTE FROM THE CANDIDATE
I know an internship like this usually expects strong organization and the ability to keep timelines straight across several workstreams at once. That is honestly the exact skill I most want to sharpen this summer, because between my rotations, the research assistantship, and the QI project I have been juggling several things at the same time, and while I have kept all of them moving, I know I can get a lot sharper about cleanly sequencing them and keeping each one's record separate and precise.
jd: Northbridge Health is offering a summer Clinical Quality-Improvement Internship for a pre-licensure nursing student. The intern will support our patient-safety and quality team on an active fall-prevention initiative across two medical-surgical units. Responsibilities include assisting with chart audits against a standardized abstraction tool, helping maintain run charts and audit logs, observing nursing workflow, and supporting the team in preparing a small end-of-summer findings readout. This is a learning role: we expect you to ask questions and work under close supervision. We are looking for a candidate who can keep an accurate, well-sequenced record of their work, follow a data-collection protocol faithfully, and clearly explain what they did, in what order, and why. Prior exposure to quality improvement, chart review, or run charts is a plus but not required. CITI human-subjects training (or willingness to complete it before starting) is required.

history.q: Thanks for coming in. Tell me about yourself and why this quality-improvement internship in particular.
Your resume mentions a hand-hygiene QI project. Walk me through what you personally did on that project, start to finish.
Got it. Separately, tell me about the faculty chart-review study — what's your actual role there?
I want to understand sequencing. Across your telemetry rotation, the chart-review study, and the hand-hygiene project — which came first, and how did they overlap?
Let's nail down the hand-hygiene project specifically. When did you collect your observations relative to when you presented the run chart, and when did the glove-box idea come up?

candidate_last_answer: Right, so — okay, so the hand-hygiene thing, I think we collected the observations after we presented, no wait, we presented the run chart and then went back and got more data, because the educator wanted a bigger sample. And actually that kind of ran at the same time as the chart-review study, which started, I want to say, before my telemetry rotation? But the telemetry rotation is also where I got interested in readmissions, so maybe it was after. And then on the CNA side at Maplewood I was also doing charting all summer, which honestly taught me more than any of it, and the anatomy tutoring was that same semester too — I was running review sessions while collecting the hand-hygiene observations, so it was all kind of happening together. The glove-box idea, I think that came first actually, before we even had the data, because someone just noticed the boxes were in a weird spot.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: I think we collected the observations after we presented, no wait

expected-yield: force the candidate to commit to a single, consistent chronology of the hand-hygiene project alone — when observations were collected, when the run chart was presented, and when the glove-box intervention was proposed — separating it cleanly from the other workstreams she keeps merging in
